title: The Macro Minute: Will the Kevin Warsh Fed be friend or foe in the years to come?

Happy Thursday out there team 42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Daleburg in
our macro minute for Thursday, July
16th, 2026. As always, we start with the
executive summary from today's lead-off
morning note. So, let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, will the
Kevin Warsh Fed be friend or foe in the
years to come?
The short answer is, it depends on who
or what you are. Winners likely include
any person or entity on board with
quashing the K-shaped monetary policy
drivers of the socially destabilizing
wealth pump, which is the category
defining term that Dr. Peter Turchin and
his colleagues at Crisis DB used to
describe the K-shaped fiscal policy,
K-shaped monetary policy, and K-shaped
regulatory policy that elites have
increasingly used over the past 50 years
to siphon income and wealth from the
bottom 80 to 90% of the population to
the top 10 to 20%.
Losers likely include any person or
entity whose sources of income and
wealth demand resistance to any policy
that attempts to pivot the US's K-shaped
economy to an E-shaped economy. Refer to
our June 12th lead-off morning note for
more details, which we published
broadly. You can check that out. I think
I published it to Twitter and LinkedIn
as well. So, hopefully everyone gets
their hands on that and and and passes
it around cuz I think we're doing the
Lord's work here. So, anyway, getting
back to the research. In terms of
analytical nuance, here are the most
important signals that we acquired or
had confirmed regarding the evolving
structural outlook for US monetary
policy in Chair Warsh's 2 hours of
testimony yesterday to the Senate
Banking Committee. Number one,
consistent with our Bayesian prior, the
historically unaccountable groupthink
that caused the Fed to quote look
through and outright blame supply shocks
for persistent failure to achieve its
price stability mandate is likely in the
rearview mirror. In the context of the
inflationary structural disintegration
of the geopolitical world order, this
dynamic represents structural right tail
risk for the US dollar and Treasury
bonds. Conversely, it represents
structural left tail risk for risk
assets and gold. Number two, consistent
with our Bayesian prior, the Fed would
likely pivot its reaction function to
focusing more on real focusing on more
real-time measures of inflation, which
have lagged realized inflation in recent
years. This outcome has dovish policy
implications. Number three, Chair
Warsh's view that the positive demand
shock from AI CapEx is not inflationary,
even if it causes temporary increase in
prices. Sorry, my apologies. Chair
Warsh's view is that the positive demand
shock from AI CapEx is not inflationary
even if it causes a temporary increase
in prices, because all of the investment
will likely lead to a positive supply
shock down the road. This view has
dovish policy implications. We'll skip
ahead here. Just jumping to number eight
and then we'll conclude with number 11.
Number eight, consistent with our
Bayesian prior, Chair Warsh is not on
board with paradigm D, which is the
print phase of the cut, grow, print menu
of treatment options designed to treat
the US's sovereign debt disease and the
market disequilibrium that it causes,
which is paradigm A. We'll call it
paradigm B as the cut phase, paradigm C,
the current phase is the grow phase, and
paradigm D, which is the print phase.
This him not being on board with
paradigm D, the print phase, all but
guarantees we'll be right on our
Bayesian prior that the Fed will seek to
deregulate US commercial banks in a way
that passes the hot potato of marketable
Treasury supply exclusively to large
banks, which typically and
overwhelmingly serve large customers.
This will allow the Fed to focus
incremental deregulation efforts on
small banks, which would accelerate the
credit impulse in the real economy,
according to the similar research of Dr.
Richard Werner. And then finally,
shifting to a number 11,
Chair Warsh is not a fan of K-shaped
monetary policy, which is ultimately
what paradigm D, the print phase,
represents. While it is highly unlikely
that he'll ever communicate this with
such blunt terminology, he is saying all
the right things to those of us who are
listening. Thus, it is likely that he
will seek to delay the advent of
paradigm D by doing everything in his
power to perpetuate paradigm C, the
growth phase. Those of us who assume
this automatically translates to dovish
monetary policy may be disappointed.
Why? Because play-action passing, i.e.
tightening cyclically to set up the run,
i.e. easing structurally, creates the
maximum amount of scope to prolong the
business cycle for a central bank that
is currently projecting to miss its own
inflation target for seven consecutive
years.
As always, I'll wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled
"Kiss Future Treasury Bonds
Treasury bonds for international users."
It says, "Hi, based on the recent
lead-off morning note, it seems that the
it seems the view is Treasury bonds may
be coming back to kiss. As a newest
subscriber, I've only known equity,
gold, and Bitcoin, that version of kiss.
As an international UK investor, can I
ask so that I can be prepared what to
what what expected Treasury bonds may
look like, i.e. duration? Would one
expect long, intermediate, or short-term
bonds or an act type so that I can look
for UK-based ETFs that track US bonds
albeit with currency exchange risk?
Alternatively, I could buy UK gilts to
remove FX risk, but again, the duration
or blend would be helpful to know. Any
thoughts to get prepared, please?" So,
great question. Now, this is a question
I've been thinking about internally for
months now ever since the first big
drawdown we saw on gold going back to
January 30th when the Kevin Warsh
announcement was made for you know, for
when when when Kevin Warsh was
officially nominated for Fed chair. We
recall that gold was down over 10% that
day, silver was down over 20% that day.
I think those were two of the more
sizable declines that we'd ever seen in
those asset classes day over day. I'll
never forget it because it was the day
that I lost the maximum amount of money
in my portfolio as an investor having
been doing this for for two decades. So,
>> [clears throat]
>> so anyway,
in my opinion, I think that was a big
wake-up call. That was a signal that
hey, this this this this expectation in
the marketplace that we're going to
gradually move towards paradigm D with
no interruption or an emission
is maybe a mis- maybe a misguided
expectation and maybe that's what
Bitcoin has been signaling since last
fall. Albeit, I do believe that Bitcoin
has been challenged by some of the more
idiosyncratic endogenous factors within
that asset class, namely the over
application of leverage from some of the
you know, some of the Bitcoin reserve
companies. I won't name names. Anyway,
so going back to the question, you know,
so when we last prior to
to replacing
our 30% target allocation to bonds with
gold back in October of 2024 in case we
featured AG, which is the core US
aggregate bond fund. I believe it had a
duration somewhere around 5 and 1/2 to 6
and 1/2 years. And so if we pivot back
to bonds, which I'm not saying we are
doing that anytime soon. I don't think
it's appropriate to do that anytime soon
because we ultimately think that that is
play action passing to set up the run
and if they set up the run easing
structurally, that will be positive for
gold. That will be positive for Bitcoin
even if we delay the onset of paradigm
D. So you know, I I don't know if we
know the answers yet, but I you know,
again, this is why we rely on their
institutional grade risk management
overlays that apply the ball targeting
and dynamic position sizing to kiss and
Dr. Mo as opposed to what's going on in
my brain because what's going on in my
brain, albeit incredibly well
researched, again, I think I published
more research than any human being on
global Wall Street on a on a daily,
weekly, and monthly basis.
Um
what's going on in my head is not is not
is not appropriate enough to manage
risk with in my opinion. You know, and
again,
incredibly informed of views. So just
getting back into the question,
you know, if we did pivot that 30%
target allocation to gold
back to bonds, we would move gold down.
We would change the Bitcoin allocation
most likely. Put you know, kind of
downgrade gold to the 10% target
allocation and then ultimately swap in
something like an international
aggregate. I believe BNDX
is the ticker for that uh of the US
aggregate. Yeah, we want to not be
taking factor risk from a geographic
standpoint uh in case uh going forward,
which is consistent with the move we
made to swap out the S&P 100 for VT
Global Equities uh the global equity
market um uh uh
uh I want to say back in February of
this year. So, uh you know, again, I'm
previewing that, but I'm not saying it's
imminent. It's a highly likely uh highly
unlikely uh to be imminent uh in our
opinion. We have a lot more data uh to
gather. We have a lot more data to
analyze and ultimately uh we have a lot
more policy signals to to digest uh
between uh now uh and let's call it the
next maybe, you know, three, four
quarters uh to to really get a genuine
sense of of if this is the appropriate
strategy to make that pivot back to
bonds in case. So, we'll wrap it up
there. Darius here presenting our Macro
Minute uh for Thursday, July 16th, 2026.
Best of luck out there today. Uh I think
uh we're going to have some scheduling
conflicts tomorrow, so we will catch you
back here on Monday. Uh everyone have a
wonderful weekend. Cheers.
